Jaipur.name: Hawa Mahal
Jaipur.description: The iconic Palace of Winds is a five-storey pink sandstone facade with 953 latticed windows. Built in 1799 by Maharaja Sawai Pratap Singh, it allowed royal ladies to observe street life while remaining unseen. The intricate honeycomb architecture is a masterpiece of Rajput craftsmanship.

Jaipur.name: Amer Fort
Jaipur.description: A magnificent hilltop fort blending Rajput and Mughal architecture, offering panoramic views of Maota Lake. The Sheesh Mahal (Mirror Palace) inside dazzles with thousands of tiny mirror mosaics. Elephant rides to the fort gates are a popular attraction.

Jaipur.name: City Palace
Jaipur.description: A magnificent royal complex in the heart of the old city comprising courtyards, gardens, and museums. The opulent Mubarak Mahal and Maharani Palace display royal garments and weapons. The palace is still partially home to the Jaipur royal family.

Jaipur.name: Jal Mahal
Jaipur.description: The Water Palace floats serenely in the middle of Man Sagar Lake, creating one of Jaipur's most photographed scenes. Built in 1799, this five-storey palace has four floors submerged under water. The surrounding lake is a sanctuary for migratory birds.

Jaipur.name: Nahargarh Fort
Jaipur.description: Perched on the rugged Aravalli hills, Nahargarh Fort offers sweeping sunset views over the Pink City. Built in 1734 by Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II, the fort features a series of interconnected palaces. It's a favourite for travellers seeking dramatic skyline photography.

Jaipur.name: Albert Hall Museum
Jaipur.description: Rajasthan's oldest museum, housed in a stunning Indo-Saracenic building at the centre of Ram Niwas Garden. The collection includes Egyptian mummies, miniature paintings, carpets, sculptures, and ivory art. A must-visit for history and art enthusiasts.

Jaipur.name: Jantar Mantar
Jaipur.description: This UNESCO World Heritage astronomical observatory contains the world's largest stone sundial and 19 geometric instruments built in the 18th century. Maharaja Jai Singh II designed it to make precise astronomical observations. The giant Samrat Yantra sundial is accurate to 2 seconds.

Jaipur.name: Birla Mandir
Jaipur.description: A beautiful white marble temple dedicated to Lord Vishnu and Goddess Lakshmi, built in 1988 by the Birla Foundation. The temple glows brilliantly in the evening against the Moti Dungri hill backdrop. Its walls are elegantly carved with scenes from Hindu mythology.

Delhi.name: India Gate
Delhi.description: A solemn war memorial standing 42 metres tall at the eastern end of Kartavya Path, dedicated to 82,000 Indian soldiers who died in World War I. The eternal flame of Amar Jawan Jyoti burns beneath the arch. The surrounding lawns are a popular picnic spot for Delhiites.

Delhi.name: Red Fort
Delhi.description: This UNESCO World Heritage Site is a magnificent Mughal fortress built in 1648 by Emperor Shah Jahan. Its massive red sandstone walls enclose palaces, museums, and the iconic Lahori Gate. Every year, India's Prime Minister addresses the nation from its ramparts on Independence Day.

Delhi.name: Qutub Minar
Delhi.description: The world's tallest brick minaret at 72.5 metres, begun in 1193 CE by Qutb ud-Din Aibak. This UNESCO World Heritage complex includes the Quwwat-ul-Islam Mosque and the mysterious Iron Pillar that has stood rust-free for 1600 years. A pinnacle of Indo-Islamic architecture.

Delhi.name: Humayun's Tomb
Delhi.description: The first garden-tomb on the Indian subcontinent, this UNESCO World Heritage Site inspired the design of the Taj Mahal. Built in 1570 by Emperor Humayun's widow, it showcases Persian architectural influence. The symmetrical Mughal gardens surrounding it are equally breathtaking.

Delhi.name: Lotus Temple
Delhi.description: A stunning Bahá'í House of Worship shaped like a blooming lotus flower with 27 marble-clad petals. Open to all faiths, it welcomes over 4 million visitors annually making it one of the most-visited buildings in the world. The surrounding pools and gardens add to its ethereal beauty.

Delhi.name: Akshardham Temple
Delhi.description: One of the world's largest Hindu temples, this spiritual-cultural campus is adorned with intricate carvings of deities, animals, and dancers. Built in just 5 years and inaugurated in 2005, it features an IMAX film about Swaminarayan's life and a stunning water show every evening.

Delhi.name: Jama Masjid
Delhi.description: India's largest mosque, built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan between 1644 and 1656. The courtyard can hold up to 25,000 worshippers for Friday prayers. Its three great gates, four towers, and two 40-metre minarets dominate the Old Delhi skyline.

Delhi.name: Rashtrapati Bhavan
Delhi.description: The official residence of the President of India and one of the largest presidential residences in the world with 340 rooms. Designed by Sir Edwin Lutyens, the building blends European Neoclassical and Indian architectural styles. The Mughal Gardens inside bloom magnificently in spring.

Delhi.name: Connaught Place
Delhi.description: The vibrant commercial and financial hub of New Delhi, built in a grand circular Georgian-style architecture. The colonnaded outer rings house restaurants, shops, and cinemas in a series of radial roads. It is the premier destination for shopping, dining, and nightlife in the capital.

Agra.name: Taj Mahal
Agra.description: One of the Seven Wonders of the World and a UNESCO World Heritage Site, the Taj Mahal is an eternal symbol of love built by Mughal Emperor Shah Jahan in memory of his wife Mumtaz Mahal. This white marble mausoleum changes colour with the light — pink at dawn, white at noon, and golden at sunset.

Agra.name: Agra Fort
Agra.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site, this massive Mughal fort in red sandstone served as the main residence of the emperors until 1638. The fort contains Jahangir's Palace, the Pearl Mosque, and the Diwan-i-Khas. From Musamman Burj tower, you can see the Taj Mahal across the Yamuna River.

Agra.name: Fatehpur Sikri
Agra.description: A ghost city and UNESCO World Heritage Site built by Emperor Akbar in 1571 and abandoned just 14 years later due to water scarcity. The perfectly preserved Mughal city includes the Buland Darwaza (largest gateway in the world), Panch Mahal, and the ornate tomb of Salim Chishti.

Agra.name: Itimad-ud-Daulah
Agra.description: Often called the Baby Taj, this delicate white marble mausoleum is the tomb of Mirza Ghiyas Beg (grandfather of Mumtaz Mahal). Built between 1622 and 1628, it is the first Mughal structure built entirely of marble with intricate pietra dura inlay work — a prototype for the Taj Mahal.

Agra.name: Mehtab Bagh
Agra.description: The Moonlit Garden is positioned directly across the Yamuna River from the Taj Mahal, offering one of the most spectacular and uncrowded views of the Taj at sunset and moonrise. The perfectly aligned garden enhances the Taj's reflection in the river.

Agra.name: Akbar's Tomb
Agra.description: Located in Sikandra on the outskirts of Agra, this sandstone and marble mausoleum is the final resting place of Emperor Akbar, built partly by himself and completed by his son Jahangir. The multi-tiered structure blends Hindu, Buddhist, Jain, and Islamic architectural elements.

Mumbai.name: Gateway of India
Mumbai.description: A triumphant arch overlooking the Arabian Sea, built to commemorate the visit of King George V in 1911. The 26-metre basalt monument blends Indo-Saracenic and Roman arch styles. It's the departure point for ferry rides to Elephanta Island and Alibaug.

Mumbai.name: Elephanta Caves
Mumbai.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site consisting of rock-cut cave temples on Elephanta Island, dedicated to Lord Shiva. The stunning 6-metre Trimurti sculpture depicting Shiva's three aspects is the centrepiece. Reachable by a scenic one-hour ferry ride from the Gateway of India.

Mumbai.name: Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus
Mumbai.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site and architectural masterpiece, this Victorian Gothic railway terminal was built in 1887 during the British Raj. Its ornate stone carvings, gargoyles, pointed arches, and central dome make it one of Mumbai's most iconic landmarks. It handles over 3 million commuters daily.

Mumbai.name: Marine Drive
Mumbai.description: The famous 3.6-kilometre coastal boulevard of Mumbai, affectionately called the Queen's Necklace for its sparkling arc of street lights visible from above at night. Art Deco buildings line the seafront promenade, and evenings here offer a spectacular view of the Arabian Sea sunset.

Mumbai.name: Siddhivinayak Temple
Mumbai.description: One of Mumbai's most revered Hindu temples, dedicated to Lord Ganesha and visited by millions of devotees, including Bollywood celebrities and politicians. The original temple was built in 1801 and expanded with a gold-plated dome and a black stone idol of Ganesha. Tuesday mornings draw massive crowds.

Mumbai.name: Juhu Beach
Mumbai.description: Mumbai's most popular beach, stretching 6 kilometres along the Arabian Sea. A favourite escape for Mumbaikars with a lively street food culture offering pav bhaji, bhel puri, and vada pav. The beach comes alive at sunset with vendors, camels, and the city's infectious energy.

Mumbai.name: Haji Ali Dargah
Mumbai.description: An iconic mosque and dargah located on a tiny islet off Worli, connected to the mainland by a narrow 500-metre causeway that disappears at high tide. Built in 1431, the shrine of Muslim saint Haji Ali Shah Bukhari is revered across faiths. The sea-surrounded walk to the dargah is breathtaking.

Mumbai.name: Chowpatty Beach
Mumbai.description: A lively urban beach at the northern end of Marine Drive, famous for Mumbai's best street food including bhel puri, kulfi, and coconut water. The beach hosts the grand Ganesh Chaturthi immersion procession each year in spectacular fashion. Ideal for an evening stroll with iconic city skyline views.

Udaipur.name: City Palace Udaipur
Udaipur.description: The largest palace complex in Rajasthan, built over 400 years by successive rulers of the Mewar Dynasty beside the shimmering Lake Pichola. A blend of Rajput, Mughal, and European architectural styles, the museum inside displays royal artefacts, paintings, and the spectacular Mor Chowk peacock mosaics.

Udaipur.name: Lake Pichola
Udaipur.description: An artificial freshwater lake created in 1362, considered one of the most romantic lakes in India. The evening boat ride offers stunning views of the illuminated City Palace, Jag Mandir Island, and the floating Lake Palace Hotel. The ghats around the lake are perfect for sunset watching.

Udaipur.name: Jag Mandir
Udaipur.description: An island palace on Lake Pichola that once sheltered the Mughal Prince Khurram (later Emperor Shah Jahan) during his rebellion against his father. Built in the 17th century, it features ornate marble pavilions, elephant statues, and terraced gardens. Accessible by boat, it offers breathtaking lake views.

Udaipur.name: Saheliyon-ki-Bari
Udaipur.description: The Garden of the Maidens, a beautiful ornamental garden built for the 48 handmaidens who accompanied a royal princess. It features elegant fountains, marble pavilions, lotus pools, and a decorative elephant statue. A serene retreat showcasing the refined aesthetic of the Mewar rulers.

Udaipur.name: Jagdish Temple
Udaipur.description: A large Hindu temple dedicated to Lord Vishnu, built in 1651 AD during the reign of Maharana Jagat Singh I. The 24-metre high Indo-Aryan spire (shikhara) dominates the skyline of the old city. Its walls are ornately carved with elephants, dancing women, and horsemen.

Udaipur.name: Kumbhalgarh Fort
Udaipur.description: A UNESCO World Heritage Site built by Rana Kumbha in the 15th century, Kumbhalgarh is home to the world's second-longest wall after the Great Wall of China, stretching 36 kilometres. The fort contains 360 temples and the birthplace shrine of Maharana Pratap.

Bangalore.name: Lalbagh Botanical Garden
Bangalore.description: A 240-acre botanical garden established by Hyder Ali in 1760 and expanded by Tipu Sultan, housing over 1800 species of plants. The iconic Victorian glass conservatory, modelled on London's Crystal Palace, hosts biannual flower shows. The serene lake inside is perfect for morning walks.

Bangalore.name: Bangalore Palace
Bangalore.description: A magnificent palace built in 1887 inspired by England's Windsor Castle, with Tudor-style architecture featuring fortified towers, turreted parapets, and elegant arches. The sprawling interiors display oil paintings, furniture, photographs, and weapons of the Wodeyar dynasty.

Bangalore.name: ISKCON Temple Bangalore
Bangalore.description: One of the largest ISKCON temples in the world, built in 1997 with stunning white granite and marble architecture. The temple complex is dedicated to Radha Krishnachandra and features three beautifully decorated shrines. The evening aarti is an uplifting spiritual experience.

Bangalore.name: Cubbon Park
Bangalore.description: A 300-acre green lung in the heart of Bengaluru, established in 1870 by Richard Sankey. Home to thousands of trees across 68 species, the park contains the State Library, two museums, and the High Court building. A beloved jogging and leisure destination for Bangaloreans.

Bangalore.name: Tipu Sultan's Summer Palace
Bangalore.description: An exquisite example of Indo-Islamic architecture built in 1791 as the summer retreat of Tipu Sultan, the Tiger of Mysore. The two-storey teak-wood palace features intricately carved pillars and ornate arched balconies decorated with floral motifs.

Bangalore.name: Vidhana Soudha
Bangalore.description: The impressive seat of the Karnataka state legislature, built in 1956 in Neo-Dravidian style. The building is illuminated in golden light every Sunday and on public holidays, creating one of Bangalore's most iconic sights. A symbol of South Indian civic pride.

Bangalore.name: Bull Temple
Bangalore.description: The 16th-century Nandi Bull Temple in Basavanagudi houses one of the largest Nandi (sacred bull) statues in the world, carved from a single granite rock standing 4.6 metres tall. Built by Kempe Gowda I, the founder of Bangalore.
